title: Clinical Labor: Tissue Donors and Research Subjects in the Global Bioeconomy By Melinda Cooper and Catherine Waldby Duke University Press. 2014. 296 pages. $24.00 paper
abstract: There is a growing literature attempting to come to grips with the broad dynamics of social change in which the contemporary biosciences are entangled. This literature frequently invokes terms like “biocapital” and “political economy”—terms that signal “something really big is going on here, and it's linked to the economy and globalization in the most profound way.” But the analyses rarely go beyond gestural or metaphorical. Cooper and Waldby's Clinical Labor is a tour-de-force exception to that trend. It is a rigorous, theoretically nuanced, yet empirically specific reinterpretation of participation in biomedicine as labor in a neo-Marxist sense. Their jumping-off point is to argue that focusing only on researchers and clinicians as the labor force casts the research subject as an inert resource that is to accept what might be thought of as the ideology of orthodox bioeconomics. They define clinical labor as “the process of material abstraction by which the abstract, temporal imperatives of accumulation are put to work at the level of the body” (12). And they trace how it is organized in assisted reproductive technology (especially gamete donation), surrogacy, and stem-cell production on the one hand and clinical trials on the other. Their account integrates (1) a historical analysis of the transformation of biological research in concert with the transition from Fordist to post-Fordist mode of production; (2) a geospatial analysis that shows the evolving global division of labor between “core” rich Western democracies and “semiperipheral” clinical labor forces in Eastern Europe, India, and China; and (3) a cultural/regulatory analysis of the conceptual transformations of the definition of labor as well as ethical research.
